perception.who_is_this_for_a: A Disney trip planner who wants to immediately set up a specific restaurant alert, with later sections for planners, passholders, and agents.
perception.who_is_this_for_b: A skeptical Disney dining user who needs to see a realistic alert, no-login reassurance, speed proof, coverage, and pricing before trusting the product.

reasoning: Candidate A does the best job exposing the actual free-alert setup task on desktop: restaurant, dates, party size, and alert channel are all visible immediately. That is a strong functional advantage. But B is stronger as the overall landing page for this diagnosis because the first viewport more cleanly establishes the safety model, official Disney handoff, realistic sample alert, and no-login reassurance. B also presents stronger proof and paid-plan differentiation across the full page: alert volume, latency, SMS/email/push examples, coverage, pricing, FAQ, and agency path are all easier to scan. On mobile, B preserves the hierarchy better; A has the CTA and trust claim, but the actual setup path is not visible and the page feels more cramped and fragmented. B should ideally make the free-alert criteria entry more immediate, but from the screenshots it better satisfies the high-trust, polished utility strategy.
blocking_visual_issue_a: Mobile does not show the restaurant/date/party setup despite the strategy depending on immediate alert setup; desktop also shows a duplicated CTA edge below the form, which makes the hero feel slightly broken.
blocking_visual_issue_b: The first viewport CTA is clear but abstract; it does not visibly expose restaurant/date/party criteria entry, so users cannot confirm the setup mechanics before clicking.
